title: Direction-selective intertwined charge, orbital, and lattice orders under uniaxial strain in hole-doped manganite: La0.75Ca0.25MnO3
authors: Ju Hyeon Lee, Beom Hyun Kim, Bongjae Kim
abstract: The complex interplay of charge, spin, orbital, and lattice degrees of freedom governs emergent phases in quantum materials, making strain a powerful control parameter. Recent advances in free-standing layer techniques have enabled extreme strains of nearly 8%, opening access to novel and often unexpected electronic and magnetic phases. Here, using a density functional theory approach, we investigate the effect of direction-selective uniaxial strain on the prototypical Jahn-Teller system La1-xCaxMnO3 (x = 0.25). We find that different strain directions stabilize qualitatively distinct structural, charge, and orbital responses, rather than merely different strengths of the same phase. In particular, extreme uniaxial strain selectively induces cooperative Jahn-Teller, breathing-like, and site-selective modulations, thereby enabling previously inaccessible intertwined orders in manganites. These results establish direction-selective uniaxial strain as a powerful and selective route for engineering emergent phases in quantum materials.
summary: This paper demonstrates that applying uniaxial strain in specific crystallographic directions can selectively induce unique, intertwined charge, orbital, and lattice orders in manganites. It shows that strain direction, rather than just magnitude, is a critical parameter for engineering new electronic and magnetic phases in correlated oxides.
main_problem: Investigating whether direction-selective uniaxial strain can act as a qualitative tuning knob to stabilize previously inaccessible, intertwined electronic and magnetic phases in hole-doped manganites.
main_result: Different strain directions stabilize qualitatively distinct phases, such as cooperative Jahn-Teller, breathing-like, and site-selective modulations, with diagonal strain (110) specifically stabilizing a new CF-type order.
method: Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations using the VASP package with the PBEsol functional and Hubbard U/Hund's J corrections.
model_or_system: The prototypical Jahn-Teller system La0.75Ca0.25MnO3 (LCMO), modeled using an orthorhombic Pnma unit cell and an 8 formula unit supercell to capture structural and charge orders.
key_observables: Structural distortions (Jahn-Teller modes Q2, Q3 and breathing mode Q1), orbital polarization, charge disproportionation, and magnetic ground states (FM vs. A-type AFM).
important_parameters: Extreme uniaxial strain up to ~8-10% along (100), (010), (110), and (1-10) directions; Hubbard U = 4 eV; Hund's coupling J_H = 1 eV.
main_assumptions: The study assumes the validity of the DFT+U approach for treating Mn-3d electron correlations and focuses on the extreme strain regime enabled by free-standing layer techniques.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the phase diagram of LCMO vs doping; Fig 2 illustrates the lattice structure and schematic phase diagrams for different strain directions; Fig 3 displays the strain dependence of JT and breathing modes and octahedral rotation/tilting angles.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of strain engineering, describes the DFT methodology, presents results for different strain directions (uniaxial vs biaxial), analyzes the driving mechanism of phase transitions, and provides supplementary structural and energy data.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic phase diagram of bulk La1−xCaxMnO3 as a function of doping, x. At x = 0, cooperative Jahn- Teller (JT) order, accompanied by |3x2 −r2⟩/|3y2 −r2⟩or- bital ordering, is stabilized within an A-type antiferromag- netic (AFM) insulating phase. At x = 1/4, this orbital or- der is suppressed and the ferromagnetic (FM) metallic phase becomes the ground state. At half doping (x = 1/2), a CE- type orbital and antiferromagnetic ordered insulating phase emerges.

FIG. 2. (a) Schematic pseudocubic lattice of La0.75Ca0.25MnO3 (LCMO), indicated by dashed lines. The two inequivalent Mn sites are labeled 1 and 2. ϕ and θ denote the in-plane rotation and out-of-plane tilting angles, respectively. Purple and red spheres represent Mn and O atoms. The a, b, and c axes denote the global crystallographic axes, while x, y, and z denote the local axes of the MnO6 octahedron. (b) Schematic structural, electronic, and magnetic phase diagrams for different strain directions. Rhombus represent MnO6 units, with their shapes and sizes indicating the type and magnitude of the distortion. Red and blue indicate ferromagnetic (FM) and A-type antiferromagnetic (AFM) ground...

FIG. 3. Strain dependence of the averaged and site-difference of the asymmetric Jahn-Teller mode in (a) and (b), and of the breathing mode in (c) and (d), under uniaxial and biaxial strain. (e) and (f) show the in-plane rotation angle ϕ and out- of-plane tilting angle θ of the MnO6 octahedra, respectively, as defined in Fig. 2(a).

FIG. 4. Orbital angles for Mn sites 1 and 2, shown in (a) and (b), respectively, defined as tan−1(Q2/Q3) (see Fig. 2(a) for the site labels). The orbital characters |3x2 −r2⟩, |3y2 −r2⟩, and |x2 −y2⟩correspond to orbital angles of 120◦, 240◦, and 180◦, respectively. The black, red, green, blue, and purple curves represent biaxial strain and uniaxial strain applied along the (100), (010), (110), and (¯1¯10) directions, respec- tively.

FIG. 5. Partial charge density for the occupied eg char- acter along the c axis (Fig. 2 (b)) is presented. The strain along (100) and (010) directions has different orbital char- acters, which are |3x2 −r2⟩or |3y2 −r2⟩and |x2 −y2⟩, re- spectively. Further, the two different Mn sites have different electron occupancies. While the strain along (110) and (¯1¯10) directions has (100) and (010) directions orbital character, in between.
